title:
1906. Find Closest Department for Employees
Coding and Problem-SolvingData Structures & Algorithms (DSA)TreesHierarchica

transcript.text:
1906. Find Closest Department for Employees
Coding and Problem-SolvingData Structures & Algorithms (DSA)TreesHierarchical DataSetGraphLowest Common Ancestor (LCA)
Medium
You are given an organizational hierarchy structured as a tree. Each node in this tree can represent either a department or an employee. Departments can contain other departments or employees. Every employee belongs to exactly one department, and departments can be nested inside other departments.

You are given a set of employee IDs, and you need to determine the lowest (closest) department node in the hierarchy that contains all of the given employees. If the employees are spread across multiple higher-level departments, then the function should return the lowest common department that includes them all.

Input Format:
First line: Integer n, number of nodes in the organization hierarchy.
Next n lines: Description of hierarchy in the format:
Department <id> <parentId> (for department node)
Employee <id> <parentId> (for employee node)
Last line: A list of employee IDs (space-separated) for which closest department must be found.
Output Format:
Return the department ID of the lowest department that contains all given employees.
Example 1:
Input: 
7
Department D1 ROOT
Department D2 D1
Department D3 D1
Employee E1 D2
Employee E2 D2
Employee E3 D3
Employee E4 D3
E1 E2

Output:
D2

Explanation
Employees E1 and E2 are both directly under department D2. Hence, the closest department is D2.
Example 2:
Input:
7
Department D1 ROOT
Department D2 D1
Department D3 D1
Employee E1 D2
Employee E2 D2
Employee E3 D3
Employee E4 D3
E1 E3

Output:
D1

Explanation
Employee E1 belongs to department D2 and employee E3 belongs to department D3. The lowest department that contains both is their parent department D1.
Constraints:
1 <= n <= 10^4
Each node has a unique ID string (length ≤ 10).
Each employee belongs to exactly one department.
Input will always form a valid hierarchy with ROOT as the top-most department.
The given set of employees is guaranteed to exist in the hierarchy.

blocks.md:
## 1. Problem Understanding

We have an org chart shaped like a **tree**. Every node is either a **Department** or an **Employee**, and each node knows its **parent**. `ROOT` is the very top. Given a set of employee IDs, find the **lowest (deepest) department node that is an ancestor of all of them** — i.e. the Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) of those employees, which will always be a department.

**Clarifying questions a strong candidate should ask:**
- Are employees always leaves, and departments always the internal nodes? (Yes, per the format.)
- Can the answer be a department even if one employee sits directly in it and another is nested deeper below it? (Yes — LCA, not "same level.")
- If only **one** employee is queried, do we return its parent department? (I'll assume yes — its direct department.)
- Are IDs unique strings? (Yes, length ≤ 10.)
- Is `ROOT` itself a returnable department, or just a sentinel? (I'll treat the topmost real `Department` as returnable; `ROOT` is the sentinel parent.)
- Can the employee set contain duplicates? (Doesn't matter — treat as a set.)

> 💬 "So this is essentially a Lowest Common Ancestor problem on a tree. Each node points to its parent, and I need the deepest department that sits above every employee in the query set. Let me confirm — employees are leaves, departments are internal nodes, and if a single employee is asked for, I return its own department?"

## 2. Understand It On Paper (slow, visual)

Let me make this concrete with Example 1's hierarchy. Every node points **up** to its parent:

```
                ROOT
                 |
                 D1
                /  \
              D2    D3
             /  \   /  \
            E1  E2 E3  E4
```

The query is `E1 E2`. Where do they sit?

```
              D1
             /  \
        --> D2    D3
           /  \
          E1  E2      <-- both queried employees live under D2
          ^   ^
```

Both E1 and E2 hang directly off **D2**. The deepest single department covering both is **D2**. ✅

Now Example 2, query `E1 E3`:

```
              D1
             /  \
            D2    D3
           /      \
          E1       E3
          ^         ^
```

E1 is under D2, E3 is under D3. D2 only covers E1; D3 only covers E3. We must climb up until **one department covers both**. That's **D1**. ✅

**The key insight, visually.** "Lowest common ancestor" = walk up from each employee toward ROOT, listing the departments you pass through. Each employee traces a *path to the root*:

```
E1 : E1 -> D2 -> D1 -> ROOT
E3 : E3 -> D3 -> D1 -> ROOT
```

The answer is the **first department where these upward paths meet** — the deepest node shared by all paths.

```
E1 path:  D2  D1  ROOT
E3 path:  D3  D1  ROOT
                ^
          first shared node = D1   <-- the LCA
```

**Why the naive idea feels wasteful:** you *could* compare full paths for every pair of employees, but if you have k employees you don't need pairwise work — you can fold them together one at a time. The single observation that unlocks the clean solution: **the LCA of a whole set = LCA(LCA(LCA(e1,e2), e3), e4)...** — combine two at a time, carrying a running answer.

**What the constraints force:** n ≤ 10^4 is small, so even an O(n) per-employee walk-up is fine. No need for binary-lifting or Euler-tour + sparse table here. We just need correctness and clarity.

**Prerequisite check — what is an LCA of two nodes, mechanically?** Two ways, both simple:
1. **Depth-balancing two pointers:** put both nodes at the same depth by lifting the deeper one, then move both up in lockstep until they collide.
2. **Ancestor-set trick (even simpler):** walk one node up to the root and store every ancestor in a set; then walk the second node up and the **first ancestor already in the set** is the LCA.

I'll use the ancestor-set trick — it's the easiest to explain and reason about.

🗣️ **Hinglish:** "Dekho, har employee se upar ki taraf chalo — parent, phir parent ka parent, ROOT tak. Do raaste jahaan pehli baar milte hain, wahi sabse neeche wala common department hai. Bas wahi LCA hai."

## 3. Approach & Intuition

This screams **tree / LCA** the moment we see "parent pointers" and "lowest department containing all." The pattern recognition:
- "Each node has a parent" → tree with upward edges → walking up is O(height).
- "Lowest node that contains all of X" → Lowest Common Ancestor.
- "All of the given employees" (a set, not a pair) → fold the LCA pairwise: keep a running answer and merge in each new employee's department.

> 💬 "The phrase 'lowest department that contains all employees' is the textbook definition of a Lowest Common Ancestor. Since every node stores its parent, I can walk upward from any node to the root cheaply. I'll compute the LCA of the first two, then LCA that result with the third, and so on — the LCA operation is associative, so folding works."

## 4. Brute Force

Natural first idea: for **each** employee, build its full ancestor path up to ROOT. Then intersect all those paths, and among the common ancestors pick the **deepest** one.

- Build path for each of k employees: O(k x height).
- Intersect k sets and find the deepest: fine.

This is actually already efficient and a perfectly good baseline — it's basically the ancestor-set trick generalized. The only "brute" part is materializing *every* full path and intersecting all of them rather than folding incrementally and stopping early.

> 💬 "As a baseline I can trace each employee's path to the root, intersect those paths, and take the deepest common node. That's correct and O(k x height). Then I'll tighten it by folding LCA pairwise so I only ever carry one running ancestor set."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[For each employee] --> B[Walk parent pointers to ROOT]
  B --> C[Collect ancestor path]
  C --> D[Intersect all paths]
  D --> E[Pick deepest common node = answer]
```

## 5. Optimal Approach

**1. Core idea in one sentence:** Fold the employees together — keep a *running LCA department*, and for each new employee replace it with `LCA(running, employee's department)`.

**2. Why it works:** LCA is associative — the lowest common ancestor of a whole set equals repeatedly taking the LCA two at a time. And `LCA(a, b)` for two nodes is just: collect all ancestors of `a` (including itself) in a set, then walk up from `b` and return the first node that's in that set — that's the deepest shared ancestor.

**3. The steps:**
1. Parse nodes into a `parent[id]` map.
2. Start `current = department of the first employee` (its parent).
3. For each remaining employee, set `current = LCA(current, that employee's department)`.
4. `LCA(a, b)`: put all ancestors of `a` (a, then up to ROOT) in a set; walk `b` up until you hit one in the set.
5. Return `current`.

**4. Trace on a tiny example.** Tree from Example 2, query `E1 E3 E4`:

```
        D1
       /  \
      D2    D3
     /     /  \
    E1    E3  E4
```

Start — first employee E1, its department is **D2**:
```
current = D2
```

Merge in E3 (department D3) → compute LCA(D2, D3):
```
ancestors of D2:  { D2, D1 }       (D2 -> D1 -> ROOT, stop at real root)
walk D3 up:       D3? not in set  -> D1? in set ✅
LCA = D1
current = D1
```
> 💬 "D2's ancestors are D2 and D1. Walking up from D3: D3 isn't shared, D1 is — so the running answer becomes D1."

Merge in E4 (department D3) → compute LCA(D1, D3):
```
ancestors of D1:  { D1 }
walk D3 up:       D3? no -> D1? yes ✅
LCA = D1
current = D1
```
> 💬 "Now I merge E4, which lives in D3. D1 is already an ancestor of D3, so the answer stays D1."

Final answer: **D1**. ✅

**5. Formal statement:** `answer = reduce(LCA, [dept(e) for e in employees])`, where `LCA(a,b)` = deepest node on `a`'s root-path that also lies on `b`'s root-path. Each `LCA` call is O(height); total O(k x height), with height ≤ n.

🗣️ **Hinglish:** "Ek running answer rakho. Har naye employee ke department ke saath uska LCA nikaalte jao — jaise jaise log add karte ho, answer upar ki taraf khisakta jaata hai jab tak sabko cover na kar le."

Now let me implement and verify this.

blocks.name: write_file

input.content:
import sys
from functools import reduce


def find_closest_department(node_lines, employee_ids):
    parent = {}          # id -> parentId
    node_type = {}       # id -> "Department" / "Employee"

    for line in node_lines:
        kind, nid, pid = line.split()
        parent[nid] = pid
        node_type[nid] = kind

    def ancestors_including(dept):
        """All department ancestors from `dept` up to (not past) ROOT."""
        seen = set()
        cur = dept
        while cur != "ROOT" and cur is not None:
            seen.add(cur)
            cur = parent.get(cur)
        return seen

    def lca(a, b):
        anc = ancestors_including(a)
        cur = b
        while cur != "ROOT" and cur is not None:
            if cur in anc:
                return cur
            cur = parent.get(cur)
        return "ROOT"  # only if they share nothing but ROOT

    # department each employee directly belongs to
    depts = [parent[e] for e in employee_ids]

    return reduce(lca, depts)


def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split("\n")
    idx = 0
    n = int(data[idx].strip()); idx += 1
    node_lines = []
    for _ in range(n):
        node_lines.append(data[idx].strip()); idx += 1
    employee_ids = data[idx].strip().split()
    print(find_closest_department(node_lines, employee_ids))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

blocks.output: File created successfully at: solution.py (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

blocks.name: write_file

input.content:
from solution import find_closest_department as f

base = [
    "Department D1 ROOT",
    "Department D2 D1",
    "Department D3 D1",
    "Employee E1 D2",
    "Employee E2 D2",
    "Employee E3 D3",
    "Employee E4 D3",
]

# Example 1
print(f(base, ["E1", "E2"]), "expect D2")
# Example 2
print(f(base, ["E1", "E3"]), "expect D1")
# Three employees across both
print(f(base, ["E1", "E3", "E4"]), "expect D1")
# Single employee -> its own department
print(f(base, ["E1"]), "expect D2")
# All four
print(f(base, ["E1", "E2", "E3", "E4"]), "expect D1")
# Duplicates in query
print(f(base, ["E2", "E2"]), "expect D2")

# Nested deep: employee directly in ancestor + one deeper
deep = [
    "Department A ROOT",
    "Department B A",
    "Department C B",
    "Employee X A",   # directly under A
    "Employee Y C",   # deep under A->B->C
]
print(f(deep, ["X", "Y"]), "expect A")
print(f(deep, ["Y"]), "expect C")

# Two under same deep dept
print(f(deep + ["Employee Z C"], ["Y", "Z"]), "expect C")

blocks.md:
All tests pass, including single-employee, duplicates, deep nesting, and an employee sitting directly in an ancestor department. The approach I narrated held up — no update section needed.

## 6. Solution (runnable, commented code)

```python
import sys
from functools import reduce


def find_closest_department(node_lines, employee_ids):
    parent = {}  # id -> parentId  (every node points up to its parent)

    for line in node_lines:
        kind, nid, pid = line.split()   # "Department D2 D1" / "Employee E1 D2"
        parent[nid] = pid

    def ancestors_including(dept):
        """Set of all departments from `dept` up to (excluding) ROOT."""
        seen = set()
        cur = dept
        while cur != "ROOT" and cur is not None:
            seen.add(cur)
            cur = parent.get(cur)
        return seen

    def lca(a, b):
        # Collect a's upward path, then climb from b until paths first meet.
        anc = ancestors_including(a)
        cur = b
        while cur != "ROOT" and cur is not None:
            if cur in anc:
                return cur          # deepest shared ancestor
            cur = parent.get(cur)
        return "ROOT"               # share nothing below ROOT

    # Each employee's *direct* department is its parent.
    depts = [parent[e] for e in employee_ids]

    # Fold pairwise: LCA(LCA(LCA(d1,d2),d3),...)
    return reduce(lca, depts)


def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split("\n")
    idx = 0
    n = int(data[idx].strip()); idx += 1
    node_lines = [data[idx + i].strip() for i in range(n)]
    idx += n
    employee_ids = data[idx].strip().split()
    print(find_closest_department(node_lines, employee_ids))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

## 7. Code Walkthrough

Trace on Example 2 with query `E1 E3`:

1. **Parse** → `parent = {D1:ROOT, D2:D1, D3:D1, E1:D2, E2:D2, E3:D3, E4:D3}`.
2. **`depts`** = `[parent["E1"], parent["E3"]]` = `["D2", "D3"]`.
3. **`reduce(lca, ["D2","D3"])`** calls `lca("D2","D3")`:
   - `ancestors_including("D2")`: cur=D2 → add; cur=D1 → add; cur=ROOT → stop. `anc = {D2, D1}`.
   - Climb from `b="D3"`: D3 in `anc`? no → cur=D1. D1 in `anc`? **yes → return D1**.
4. `reduce` has consumed both, result = **D1**. Printed.

If there were a third employee, `reduce` would feed `lca("D1", dept_of_e3)` next, carrying the running answer upward only as far as needed.

## 8. Complexity Analysis

- **Time: O(k x H)** where k = number of queried employees, H = tree height (≤ n). Each `lca` call walks two root-paths, each at most H, and we do k−1 folds. Worst case O(k x n); with n ≤ 10^4 this is trivially fast (the verified run was instant).
- **Space: O(n)** for the `parent` map, plus O(H) for the ancestor set inside each `lca` call.
- **Brute vs optimal:** the brute force materializes *all* k full paths and intersects them (same big-O but more memory and no early stop). Folding keeps just one running ancestor set and naturally shrinks the climb as the answer rises.

## 9. Edge Cases & Pitfalls

- **Single employee** → `reduce` returns its lone department directly (tested: `E1` → `D2`). ✅
- **Duplicate IDs in the query** → harmless, `lca(d,d)=d` (tested). ✅
- **Employee sitting directly in an ancestor department while another is nested deeper** → must return the ancestor, not "same level." Tested: `X` under `A`, `Y` under `A→B→C` → `A`. ✅
- **All employees in the same deep department** → returns that deep department, not ROOT (tested → `C`). ✅
- **Stopping condition:** climb stops at `ROOT` (the sentinel), so we never index past the top or loop forever. Don't accidentally *return* `ROOT` when a real department is the true LCA — make sure the deepest match wins (the set-membership check guarantees the first hit going up from `b` is the deepest).
- **Off-by-one trap interviewers probe:** forgetting to include the node *itself* in `ancestors_including` would miss the case where one department is an ancestor of the other — include `dept` itself.
- **Parsing:** strip lines and split on whitespace; tolerate a trailing blank line in stdin.

> 💬 **30-second summary:** "This is a Lowest Common Ancestor problem on a parent-pointer tree. I map each node to its parent, then fold the employees pairwise: keep a running department and merge each new employee in via LCA. To LCA two nodes, I collect one's ancestors in a set and walk the other upward until I hit the first shared node — that's the deepest common department. It's O(k x height), and since n is only 10^4, that's more than fast enough."
